title: Vector Custom App API — Design Investigation
topic: Rally App SDK 2.1 API architecture review for designing a sandboxed custom-app API in Vector
summary: Deep analysis of Rally's App SDK 2.1 to inform Vector's own sandboxed custom-app API, covering models, filters, user/subscription scoping, and role-gating design.
content:
Executive Summary

This report investigates Rally (CA Agile Central) App SDK 2.1 as a reference architecture for giving Vector users the ability to build custom applications, widgets, reports, and data visualisations against Vector platform data. Rally is the closest published precedent for a B2B SaaS product that exposes a scoped, sandboxed JavaScript SDK to its own registered users. The goal is to distil the patterns that work, adapt them to Vector's role model (user / padmin / gadmin), and define the governing rules of a Vector App SDK — hereafter referred to as Samantha, the internal name for Vector's custom-app layer.

1. Rally App SDK — Architecture Overview

Rally's SDK is a JavaScript framework built on top of Sencha Ext JS. Developers write apps in JavaScript, and those apps are deployed inside custom pages within a user's Rally subscription. Key architectural decisions:

  • Host-page sandbox: Apps do not run standalone. They are embedded inside a Rally custom page, which acts as both the container and the security perimeter. The host page owns authentication and context injection.
  • Context injection at launch: Every app receives a Rally.app.Context object at construction time. This context is the single source of truth for who is running the app and what they are allowed to see. It is not user-constructable — the host injects it.
  • Dot-notation namespace: The entire SDK is organised as a deep dot-notation hierarchy: Rally.data.wsapi.Model, Rally.domain.User, Rally.app.App, etc. Every class lives in a predictable namespace path that signals its layer and responsibility.
  • Promise-based async: All data operations return Deft.Promise objects. Callbacks (success, failure) are also supported for backwards compatibility.
  • Two data APIs: WSAPI (the standard REST API for current data) and the Lookback API (MongoDB-style query engine for historical snapshots). The SDK wraps both behind consistent store/filter abstractions.

2. The Five Core Classes — Detailed Breakdown

2a. Rally.data.wsapi.Model

What it is: The base record object. When you query Rally for a User Story, Defect, or any work item, you get back instances of Rally.data.wsapi.Model. Think of it as a typed row from the database, with field-level awareness built in.

File hierarchy: src/data/wsapi/Model.js — extends Rally.data.Model (base), which in turn extends Ext.data.Model (Sencha).

Method / PropertyTypeDescription
uriKeyProperty (String)Key on the record that stores its resource URI. Defaults to '_ref'.
getUri()Instance methodReturns the resource URI for this record (e.g. /hierarchicalrequirement/12345).
getField(fieldName)Instance + StaticReturns the field descriptor object for a named field.
hasField(field)Instance + StaticReturns boolean — does this model have the named field?
isFieldVisible(fieldName)InstanceReturns true if the field exists and is not hidden.
getFields()Instance + StaticReturns all field descriptor objects.
getDirtyFields()InstanceReturns only fields that have been modified since last commit.
hasValue(field)InstanceReturns true if the field has a non-null, non-empty value.
isCustomField(fieldName)InstanceReturns false on the base class; overridden by subtypes to identify custom fields.
commit(silent)InstanceCommits changes and fires the aftercommit event.
getCustomFields()StaticReturns only the custom (user-defined) fields on the model.
isAttributeEqual(attr, value)StaticReturns a predicate function — useful for filter construction.
addField(field)StaticAdds a field definition to the model class.

Key insight: Models are schema-aware. They know their own fields, which are visible, which are custom, and whether they've been changed. This is what allows Rally's store layer to do smart dirty-checking before issuing saves.

2b. Rally.data.lookback.QueryFilter

What it is: The filter builder for the Lookback (historical snapshot) API. Uses MongoDB-style operators ($gt, $lt, $in, $exists, $and, $or) rather than the SQL-like string syntax used by the WSAPI filter.

File hierarchy: src/data/lookback/QueryFilter.js — extends Ext.util.Filter.

Method / PropertyTypeDescription
propertyConfig (String, required)The field to query against.
operatorConfig (String, required)One of: =, !=, >, <, >=, <=, contains, in, exists.
valueConfig (Object, required)The comparison value.
constructor(config)InstanceMerges config and calls parent constructor.
and(filter)InstanceCombines this filter with another using $and. Returns a new filter.
or(filter)InstanceCombines this filter with another using $or. Returns a new filter.
toObject()InstanceSerialises the filter to a Lookback API-compatible plain object.
QueryFilter.and(queries)StaticANDs an array of filters together. Returns null if array is empty.
QueryFilter.or(queries)StaticORs an array of filters together. Returns null if array is empty.
QueryFilter.fromExtFilter(filter)StaticConverts an Ext filter into a QueryFilter.

Key insight: Both the instance and static forms of and() / or() are provided. Static forms are for building filters programmatically from collections; instance forms allow chaining. This dual pattern is elegant and worth adopting in Vector.

2c. Rally.data.ModelFactory

What it is: A singleton registry that abstracts model retrieval. Rather than knowing which internal class handles which data type, consumer code calls Rally.data.ModelFactory.getModel({ type: 'Defect' }) and the factory routes to the right implementation.

File hierarchy: src/data/ModelFactory.js — singleton, no parent class listed.

MethodTypeDescription
registerType(type, factory)StaticRegisters a single model factory for a type string.
registerTypes(types, factory)StaticRegisters a factory for multiple types at once.
getFactory(type)StaticReturns the registered factory for a normalised type string.
getModel(options)StaticFetches a single model by options.type. Returns a Deft.Promise. Also supports success / failure callbacks.
getModels(options)StaticFetches multiple models from an array of types. Batches by factory, merges results. Returns a unified promise.
_getCompatibleType(type)Private staticNormalises type string: lowercase, strip whitespace, extract base type from slash-delimited refs.
_flattenPromiseValues(values)Private staticMerges multiple factory response objects into one flat result object.
_ensureTypesAreDefined(types)Private staticValidates types array is present and non-empty; throws descriptive errors.

Key insight: The factory pattern decouples consumer code from model implementation. When Rally adds a new data type or changes an internal model class, consumer apps don't break. Vector's API should adopt this same factory indirection.

2d. Rally.domain.Subscription

What it is: A lightweight value object representing the current Rally subscription — its type, enabled modules, and edition. Obtained via context.getSubscription(); never constructed directly by app code.

File hierarchy: src/domain/Subscription.js.

Method / PropertyTypeDescription
SubscriptionTypeProperty (String)Edition identifier: 'Community', 'Express_Edition', 'Unlimited', or HS-prefixed variants.
ModulesProperty (Array)List of enabled module names.
constructor(config)InstanceApplies config via Ext.apply(this, config).
isModuleEnabled(moduleName)InstanceReturns boolean; checks if moduleName exists in Modules.
isAnyOfTheseTypes(types)InstanceReturns boolean; accepts an array of type strings, handles HS remapping.
isPaidSubscription()InstanceTrue if not Community or HS edition.
isCommunityEdition()InstanceTrue if SubscriptionType === 'Community'.
isHsEdition()InstanceTrue if SubscriptionType contains 'HS'.
isExpressEdition()InstanceTrue if SubscriptionType === 'Express_Edition'.
isUnlimitedEdition()InstanceTrue if SubscriptionType === 'Unlimited'.

Key insight: The Subscription object is purely read-only from the app developer's perspective. It tells the app what features are available but cannot be mutated. Vector should adopt the same pattern — a read-only tenant/plan context injected at app load time.

2e. Rally.domain.User

What it is: A lightweight value object representing the currently authenticated user. Obtained via context.getUser(); never constructed directly.

File hierarchy: src/domain/User.js.

Method / PropertyTypeDescription
NameProperty (String)The user's display name.
UserNameProperty (String)The user's login/email identifier.
UserPermissionsProperty (Object)Raw permissions data for the current user.
UserProfileProperty (Object)Profile metadata: default project, datetime format preferences, etc.
constructor(config)InstanceApplies config via Ext.apply(this, config).
getRef()InstanceReturns the user's resource reference (_ref).
getDisplayString()Instance (private)Returns the display name used throughout the app (_refObjectName).
getLandingPage()Instance (private)Returns the unscoped hash for the user's default landing page.

2f. Rally.auth.UserPermissions (companion class)

Though not one of the five requested pages, this class is critical to understanding Rally's scoping model and is obtained via context.getPermissions().

MethodDescription
isSubscriptionAdmin()Is the current user a subscription-level administrator?
isWorkspaceAdmin(workspaceRef)Is the user an admin of a specific workspace?
isWorkspaceAdminInAnyWorkspace()Is the user an admin in at least one workspace?
isProjectAdmin(projectRef)Is the user an admin of a specific project?
isProjectEditor(projectRef)Does the user have editor rights for a specific project?
isProjectAdminInAnyProject()Is the user a project admin anywhere in the current workspace?
isProjectAdminInAnyWorkspace()Is the user a project admin in any workspace?
isWorkspaceOrSubscriptionAdmin(workspaceRef)Combined workspace + subscription admin check.
isProjectWorkspaceOrSubscriptionAdmin()Combined three-level admin check.
hasEditorAccessToAnyProject()Does the user have editor or higher access anywhere?
isInRoleForScope(role, scopeRef)Validates a specific role at a specific scope reference.
getPermissionAtScope(scopeRef)Returns the permission object for the given scope.
getProjectToWorkspaceMap()Builds an OID-indexed map of project → workspace relationships (cached).

3. Rally App Lifecycle — The Sandbox Model

Understanding how a Rally app runs is the most important pattern for Vector to replicate:

  1. The host page loads first. Rally's custom page infrastructure authenticates the user, establishes the session, and loads the SDK.
  2. Context is built by the host, not the app. The host constructs a Rally.app.Context containing the authenticated user object, subscription object, workspace, project, and permissions. The app cannot construct or modify this context.
  3. The app's launch() method is called. This is the developer's entry point — equivalent to main(). By this point, context is fully resolved and injected.
  4. Data access is context-scoped. Stores and models inherit the context automatically. A user who only has read access to certain projects will only get data from those projects — the API enforces this server-side.
  5. Apps cannot escape their container. There is no way for app code to call Rally's REST API directly with different credentials. All data flows through SDK abstractions that carry the injected context.

4. Rally Lookback API — The Historical Data Layer

The Lookback API is Rally's second data API, designed for analytics and time-series queries. It uses a MongoDB-style query syntax and differs from WSAPI in a critical security way:

"Unlike Rally's standard API, permissions are checked after query execution. If results span projects lacking user read access, the API returns an error listing restricted projects rather than silently filtering results."

This is a deliberate design decision: fail loudly rather than silently truncate. Vector's API should adopt the same philosophy — permission failures should surface as explicit errors, not silent empty results.

5. Vector Samantha API — Design Recommendations

The following section translates the Rally architecture into concrete design decisions for Vector's custom-app SDK.

5a. Namespace & Dot-Syntax Convention

Rally's entire SDK lives under a single root namespace (Rally.*). Vector should adopt the same pattern under the already-named samantha.* root. Proposed top-level namespace tree:

samantha.app.App                     — base class all apps extend
samantha.app.Context                 — injected context (read-only to app code)
samantha.data.ModelFactory           — model registry / factory
samantha.data.wsapi.Model            — base record type
samantha.data.wsapi.Store            — primary data store
samantha.data.wsapi.Filter           — WSAPI filter builder
samantha.data.analytics.Store        — analytics / time-series store (future)
samantha.data.analytics.QueryFilter  — analytics filter builder (future)
samantha.domain.User                 — current user value object
samantha.domain.Tenant               — current tenant / plan value object (≈ Subscription)
samantha.auth.UserPermissions        — permission evaluation object
samantha.ui.chart.Chart              — chart scaffolding
samantha.ui.grid.Grid                — grid scaffolding
samantha.ui.widget.Widget            — generic widget base
samantha.util.DateTime               — date utilities
samantha.util.Ref                    — reference utilities

5b. Context Injection Pattern

Every Vector custom app must receive a samantha.app.Context at construction. The context is built by the Vector host page and is immutable from the app's perspective. It exposes:

context.getUser()           → samantha.domain.User
context.getTenant()         → samantha.domain.Tenant
context.getPermissions()    → samantha.auth.UserPermissions
context.getWorkspace()      → { id, name, ref }   (Portfolio context)
context.getProject()        → { id, name, ref }   (Team/project context, if applicable)

The app developer never constructs these objects. They are always injected.

5c. samantha.domain.User — Proposed API

Property / MethodDescription
idInternal UUID of the user.
nameDisplay name.
emailLogin email / identifier.
roleEnum: 'user' | 'padmin' | 'gadmin'.
getRef()Returns the user's canonical API reference string.
getDisplayName()Returns the name used throughout the app UI.

5d. samantha.domain.Tenant — Proposed API

Analogous to Rally's Subscription, but scoped to Vector's plan model:

Property / MethodDescription
planString: current plan tier (e.g. 'free', 'pro', 'enterprise').
featuresArray of enabled feature flag strings.
isFeatureEnabled(featureName)Returns boolean.
isPaidPlan()True if not on the free tier.

5e. samantha.auth.UserPermissions — Proposed API

Vector's three-tier role model (user / padmin / gadmin) maps onto Rally's hierarchy (project editor / project admin / subscription admin). Proposed methods:

MethodDescription
isGadmin()Is the user a global admin? Corresponds to Rally's isSubscriptionAdmin().
isPadmin()Is the user a portfolio admin? Corresponds to Rally's isWorkspaceAdmin().
isUser()Is the user an everyday member (no admin rights)?
canRead(resource)Can the user read the specified resource type?
canWrite(resource)Can the user create/update the specified resource type?
canDelete(resource)Can the user delete the specified resource type?
getRole()Returns the role string: 'user', 'padmin', or 'gadmin'.

5f. The Sandbox Wrapper

Custom apps must run inside a Vector-provided script wrapper. This wrapper is the security perimeter — it is the equivalent of Rally's custom page host. The wrapper's responsibilities:

  1. Authentication gate: The wrapper only loads if the user has an active authenticated session. No unauthenticated app execution.
  2. Context construction: The wrapper resolves the current user, tenant, and permissions from Vector's backend, then constructs the samantha.app.Context object.
  3. API surface exposure: The wrapper exposes the samantha.* namespace to the app's JavaScript scope. It does NOT expose raw fetch(), raw database access, or any internal Vector service endpoints.
  4. Origin restriction: All API calls made via the Samantha SDK are proxied through the wrapper layer. The wrapper appends authentication headers and enforces scope. Apps cannot call the Vector API with forged credentials.
  5. Permission enforcement: Data queries issued by the app are intercepted by the wrapper and annotated with the current user's permission scope before forwarding to the backend.
  6. Error surfacing: Permission failures are returned as explicit errors, not silent empty results (following the Lookback API pattern).

5g. Role-Scoping Rules

This is the governing rule set for what an app can do, based on who launches it:

RoleCan readCan writeCan administerCan launch as
userOwn data, shared portfolio items they have been granted access toOwn items onlyNoThemselves
padminAll data within their portfolio / workspacePortfolio-scoped itemsPortfolio settings, user management within portfolioThemselves; can grant apps elevated data visibility within their portfolio
gadminAll data across all tenants (system-wide)All items, all tenantsFull system administrationAny role (can impersonate for testing)

Escalation path: If a user builds an app that requires padmin-level data access (e.g. a cross-team report), the app will fail silently or return a permission error when run under a user account. The user must either: (a) ask a padmin to launch the app in their context, or (b) request an API account with explicit flag grants (a feature to be designed). This is the same pattern Rally uses — the app inherits the permissions of whoever runs it.

5h. API Account Concept (future design)

Rally does not publicly document a formal API account type, but Vector should plan for one. An API account is a non-human account with a defined permission profile. A gadmin or padmin can create an API account and assign it specific flag states (e.g. read-only on portfolio items, write on custom pages, no access to billing). Developers can then launch their apps under this API account, giving them a stable, auditable permission baseline that does not depend on a human user's session.

5i. ModelFactory Pattern for Vector

Vector's data layer should adopt the factory pattern from Rally.data.ModelFactory:

samantha.data.ModelFactory.getModel({
  type: 'PortfolioItem',
  success: function(model) {
    // model is the schema-aware class for PortfolioItem
  }
});

samantha.data.ModelFactory.getModels({
  types: ['PortfolioItem', 'TeamMember', 'WorkItem'],
  success: function(models) {
    // models.PortfolioItem, models.TeamMember, models.WorkItem
  }
});

This allows Vector to evolve internal data types without breaking existing apps.

5j. Filter Builder Pattern for Vector

Both Rally filter classes (WSAPI string-based and Lookback MongoDB-style) offer the same and() / or() chaining pattern. Vector should expose a single unified filter builder:

// Instance chaining
var filter = new samantha.data.Filter({
  property: 'status',
  operator: '=',
  value: 'active'
}).and(new samantha.data.Filter({
  property: 'owner',
  operator: '=',
  value: context.getUser().getRef()
}));

// Static composition
var filter = samantha.data.Filter.and([
  new samantha.data.Filter({ property: 'type', operator: '=', value: 'Epic' }),
  new samantha.data.Filter({ property: 'completed', operator: '!=', value: true })
]);

6. What Vector Does NOT Need to Copy

Rally's SDK was designed in 2011–2013 on top of a large Ext JS framework. Several patterns are dated and should not be replicated:

  • Ext JS dependency: Vector has no need for a framework as heavyweight as Sencha Ext JS. The Samantha SDK should be framework-agnostic — pure JavaScript with React-friendly hooks where needed.
  • Deft.Promise: Use native Promise / async-await instead.
  • Callback-based APIs: Modern JS is async/await. Callbacks are a compatibility shim Rally added; do not start there.
  • Two separate filter classes: Rally has Rally.data.wsapi.Filter and Rally.data.lookback.QueryFilter because they target two different wire formats. Vector should design a single filter abstraction from the start.

7. Sources

  1. Rally App SDK 2.1 Documentation Index — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/
  2. Rally.data.ModelFactory source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/ModelFactory.html
  3. Rally.data.wsapi.Filter source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/Filter2.html
  4. Rally.data.wsapi.Store source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/Store10.html
  5. Rally.auth.UserPermissions source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/UserPermissions.html
  6. Rally.domain.User source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/User.html
  7. Rally.domain.Subscription source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/Subscription.html
  8. Rally.app.App source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/App.html
  9. Rally.data.lookback.QueryFilter source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/QueryFilter.html
  10. Rally.data.Model base class source — https://rally1.rallydev.com/docs/en-us/saas/apps/2.1/doc/source/Model3.html
  11. Rally Lookback API Reference — https://rally1.rallydev.com/analytics/doc/
  12. Broadcom TechDocs — Building Apps for Rally — https://techdocs.broadcom.com/us/en/ca-enterprise-software/valueops/rally/rally-help/reference/extending-rally-with-apps/building-apps-for-rally.html

8. Gaps & Limitations

  • Hash-fragment URLs: The five target URLs use JS-rendered hash routing (#!). Static fetching returned the index page, not individual class pages. All class-specific data was obtained by directly fetching the underlying /source/*.html files instead.
  • Rally.data.wsapi.Model save/destroy: These methods are defined in the Ext JS parent class (Ext.data.Model), not overridden in Rally's base. They are inherited rather than Rally-specific and were therefore not documented in Rally's source pages.
  • getSubscription() implementation: The method is referenced in the Subscription class docs but its implementation lives inside Rally.app.Context, which the Context source fetch returned an Ext JS diagnostic override instead of the Rally Context class. The method is confirmed to exist and to return a Rally.domain.Subscription instance.
  • API account design: Rally has no published formal API account feature for the SDK. The recommendations in section 5h are Vector-original design, not Rally-derived.
  • Documentation age: Rally App SDK 2.1 docs were generated 13 December 2016. Some patterns may reflect decisions that have since been superseded in Rally's internal architecture.

9. Next.js — Capabilities and Ecosystem for the Vector Custom App API

Vector is built on Next.js 16 (App Router). This section maps Next.js capabilities directly to the requirements of the Samantha SDK: the sandbox wrapper, role-scoped data access, the developer authoring experience, and the security model that keeps custom apps safe for all users.

9a. What Next.js Is and Why It Matters Here

Next.js is a full-stack React framework maintained by Vercel. It runs on Node.js and supports React Server Components, Server Actions, file-based routing, and a rich set of rendering strategies. Its relevance to the Samantha SDK is twofold: (1) the Samantha SDK wrapper page is a Next.js route, and all the security controls available in Next.js apply directly to it; (2) custom apps authored by Vector users run inside that wrapper, so understanding what Next.js exposes — and what it hides — defines the boundary of what app developers can reach.

Current stable version: Next.js 16.2.4 (as of April 2026).

9b. Two Router Systems — App Router Is the Target

Next.js ships with two routing systems. Vector uses the App Router (/app directory), which is the modern system. The older Pages Router (/pages) is still supported but is not relevant to new development.

FeatureApp Router (used by Vector)Pages Router (legacy)
Default component typeReact Server ComponentsClient Components
Data fetchingasync/await in component bodygetServerSideProps, getStaticProps
MutationsServer Actions ('use server')API Routes
LayoutsNested, persistent across navigations_app.js single wrapper
StreamingNative via Suspense + RSCNot natively supported
Cachinguse cache directive + Partial PrerenderingISR via revalidate

9c. Server Components vs Client Components — The Core Mental Model

This distinction is the single most important concept for understanding how the Samantha sandbox works. Every component in the App Router is one of two types:

TypeRuns onWhen to useDirective
Server ComponentServer only; never sent to browser as JSData fetching, DB access, secrets, auth checks, anything that must stay privateDefault — no directive needed
Client ComponentBrowser (after hydration)Interactivity, state, browser APIs, event handlers, useEffect'use client' at top of file

Why this matters for the sandbox: The Samantha SDK wrapper page is a Server Component. This means it can verify the user's session, resolve permissions, and construct the Context object entirely on the server — before anything is sent to the browser. User-authored app code, however, will typically be Client Components (they need interactivity). The wrapper passes a serialised, read-only context snapshot to the app as props. The app cannot reach back through that boundary to call server-side database functions directly.

Environment poisoning protection: Next.js provides a server-only package. Any module importing it will throw a build-time error if accidentally imported in a Client Component. Vector's SDK internals (session resolution, permission checks, database queries) should import server-only to guarantee they can never leak to user-authored client-side code.

9d. File-System Routing — How the Sandbox Route Is Structured

Next.js uses file-system conventions. Every page.js file becomes a route; every layout.js wraps child routes. For the Samantha sandbox, the relevant route structure is:

app/
  store/
    apps/
      [appId]/               ← dynamic route segment
        page.js              ← the sandbox wrapper page
        layout.js            ← injects auth guard and context for all app routes
      route.js               ← API Route: app manifest fetch
  api/
    samantha/
      data/
        route.js             ← Samantha data proxy endpoint
      actions/
        route.js             ← Samantha mutation proxy endpoint

Key file conventions used by the sandbox:

FileRole in sandbox
layout.jsPersistent shell; verifies session and role; does NOT re-run on navigation (important: auth checks must happen in page or DAL, not only in layout)
page.jsThe sandbox page itself; resolves app manifest, builds Context, renders the app wrapper
loading.jsSuspense fallback shown while app loads
error.jsError boundary — catches unhandled errors from user app code
not-found.jsShown if the requested appId does not exist or the user has no access
route.jsAPI endpoints for the Samantha data and action proxies

9e. Rendering Strategies — Which One Applies to the Sandbox

Next.js supports six rendering strategies. The Samantha sandbox wrapper must use dynamic server-side rendering:

StrategyHow it worksRelevant to sandbox?
Static (SSG)Rendered at build time; same HTML for all usersNo — user identity is per-request
ISRStatic with background revalidationNo — same reason
Dynamic SSRRendered per-request on the serverYes — this is the correct strategy for the wrapper page; session and permissions are resolved fresh on every request
Client-side (CSR)Rendered in browser after JS loadsPartial — user app UI is CSR once the wrapper delivers context
Streaming + SuspenseServer sends HTML progressively as data resolvesYes — useful for progressive loading of app sections
Partial Prerendering (PPR)Static shell + streaming dynamic islandsNo — incompatible with nonce-based CSP (see section 9j)

9f. Server Actions — The Right Tool for Samantha Mutations

Server Actions are Next.js's mechanism for running server-side code directly from form submissions or button clicks — without writing a separate API route. They are marked with the 'use server' directive.

For Samantha, Server Actions are the correct mechanism for all write operations (create, update, delete) that user apps trigger. Key properties:

  • Always run on the server. No matter how they are called from client code, the function body executes on the server. This means the auth check and permission enforcement happens server-side, where it cannot be bypassed.
  • Unique, non-deterministic IDs. Next.js 15+ assigns each Server Action a unique, unpredictable ID that is rotated between builds. App developers cannot guess action IDs to forge requests.
  • Must verify auth. Server Actions are essentially public POST endpoints. The official Next.js security model explicitly requires that every Server Action call verifySession() at the top of its body before doing anything. Vector's Samantha proxy must enforce this — app code that calls a Samantha write function triggers a Server Action that checks the current user's role before touching the database.
  • Progressive enhancement. Forms backed by Server Actions work without JavaScript (important for accessibility).
  • Cache invalidation. After a mutation, Server Actions can call revalidatePath() or revalidateTag() to expire cached data so the UI reflects the change immediately.

Example of a role-checked Samantha write action:

'use server'
import { verifySession } from '@/lib/dal'
import { samanthaGuard } from '@/lib/samantha/guard'

export async function createWorkItem(formData) {
  const session = await verifySession()
  // samanthaGuard checks: does this user's role permit this resource + action?
  await samanthaGuard(session, 'WorkItem', 'write')
  // proceed with DB write
}

9g. Route Handlers — The Samantha Data Proxy API

Route Handlers (app/api/**/route.js) are Next.js's REST API layer. They support all HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS) and use the Web standard Request / Response APIs — no Express-style middleware needed.

For Samantha, Route Handlers serve as the data proxy layer: user app code calls samantha.data.Store.load(), which under the hood issues a fetch to a Vector Route Handler. That handler:

  1. Verifies the session cookie
  2. Extracts the user's role and permissions
  3. Validates the requested resource type against the user's permission scope
  4. Queries the Vector database
  5. Returns only the fields the user is allowed to see (DTO pattern)

Route Handlers also support streaming responses via ReadableStream, which enables progressive delivery of large data sets — useful for custom reports or analytics widgets.

9h. Proxy (formerly Middleware) — The First Line of Defence

As of Next.js 16, what was called Middleware is now called Proxy (file: proxy.ts at project root). It runs before every route is rendered — including API routes — and is the earliest point where requests can be intercepted.

For the Samantha sandbox, the Proxy layer handles:

  • Session presence check: If a request arrives at /store/apps/* with no valid session cookie, the Proxy redirects to the login page before the page even renders. This is an optimistic check (reads from cookie, not database) — the authoritative check happens inside the page and DAL.
  • CSP nonce generation: The Proxy generates a fresh cryptographic nonce per request, injects it into the Content-Security-Policy header, and sets it as a custom x-nonce header for the page to read. This prevents any script not bearing the nonce from executing — even if an attacker injects a script tag into user app output.
  • CORS enforcement: The Proxy can enforce allowed origins on the Samantha API endpoints, preventing cross-origin calls from other sites.
  • Role-based redirects: If a user tries to access an admin-only app, the Proxy can short-circuit with a 403 before the page incurs any rendering cost.

Critical caution: Because Proxy runs on every route including prefetched routes, Next.js docs explicitly warn that Proxy must only perform optimistic (cookie-based) checks — not database queries — to avoid performance problems. Real authoritative auth checks belong in the Data Access Layer (DAL).

9i. Session Management and the Data Access Layer (DAL)

Next.js recommends a two-layer auth model that Vector's Samantha sandbox should adopt verbatim:

  1. Proxy / Proxy (optimistic): Reads the session cookie. Redirects unauthenticated users. Fast, runs at the network edge.
  2. Data Access Layer (DAL) (secure): A verifySession() function that decrypts the JWT, optionally validates against the database, and returns the user's ID and role. Called inside every Server Component, Server Action, and Route Handler that touches data. Uses React's cache() to deduplicate calls within a single render pass.

The DAL pattern for Samantha:

import 'server-only'
import { cookies } from 'next/headers'
import { decrypt } from '@/lib/session'
import { cache } from 'react'

export const verifySession = cache(async () => {
  const cookie = (await cookies()).get('session')?.value
  const session = await decrypt(cookie)
  if (!session?.userId) redirect('/login')
  return { isAuth: true, userId: session.userId, role: session.role }
})

export const getSamanthaContext = cache(async () => {
  const session = await verifySession()
  // build samantha.app.Context equivalent from session
  return buildContext(session)
})

Session storage options: Next.js supports both stateless JWT sessions (stored in an encrypted HttpOnly cookie) and database sessions (session ID in cookie, full session in database). For Samantha, the stateless JWT approach is sufficient — the JWT payload carries the user ID and role, which is all the DAL needs for permission checks. This avoids an extra database round-trip on every request.

9j. Security Model — CSP, Sandbox Headers, and Nonces

Next.js provides first-class support for Content Security Policy (CSP) via the Proxy layer. For Samantha's sandbox page, a strict CSP is non-negotiable because user-authored JavaScript runs in the same browser context as the Vector host page.

The recommended architecture is nonce-based CSP:

  1. The Proxy generates a UUID-based nonce per request and embeds it in the CSP header: script-src 'self' 'nonce-{nonce}' 'strict-dynamic'
  2. Only scripts bearing the matching nonce are allowed to execute
  3. User app scripts are delivered through the Samantha wrapper, which controls nonce injection
  4. Injected scripts (e.g., from a malicious app payload) have no nonce and are blocked

Additional HTTP security headers for the sandbox page:

HeaderValue for sandboxEffect
Content-Security-PolicyNonce-based, frame-ancestors 'self'Blocks XSS; only Vector can frame the sandbox page
X-Frame-OptionsSAMEORIGINLegacy iframe protection
X-Content-Type-OptionsnosniffPrevents MIME sniffing attacks
Referrer-Policystrict-origin-when-cross-originLimits referrer leakage
Permissions-PolicyDisable camera, microphone, geolocation unless app declares needLimits browser API surface of user apps

Trade-off: Nonce-based CSP requires all pages to be dynamically rendered (no static shell, no PPR). This is acceptable for the sandbox page since it must resolve session and permissions per request anyway. Static marketing pages or dashboard layouts that do not host user apps can still use static rendering and simpler CSP.

The iframe alternative: ChatGPT's architecture (documented by Vercel) uses a three-layer nested iframe to isolate user apps. This provides stronger DOM isolation but breaks seven Next.js features (asset loading, client navigation, CORS for RSC, etc.). For Vector, an iframe-based model would prevent user apps from using Next.js features natively. The recommended approach is the CSP + nonce model operating within a single Next.js route, with the Samantha SDK wrapper acting as the security perimeter at the JavaScript layer rather than the DOM layer.

9k. Caching Strategy for Samantha Data

Next.js 16 introduces the use cache directive (enabled via cacheComponents: true in next.config.ts). This is the basis of Partial Prerendering (PPR) — the static shell is cached, while dynamic islands stream at request time.

For Samantha data:

Data typeCaching strategyRationale
App manifest (name, description, permissions)use cache with cacheLife('hours')Changes rarely; safe to cache per app ID
User context (role, permissions)No cache — resolved fresh per requestRole changes must take effect immediately
Portfolio data (items, metrics)use cache with cacheTag('portfolio-{userId}'); revalidated on writeCan be stale for seconds; invalidated on mutation
Real-time / live dataNo cache; wrap in <Suspense>Always fresh

Cache invalidation after a Samantha mutation uses revalidateTag(). When an app writes data, the Samantha Server Action calls revalidateTag('portfolio-{userId}'), which expires any cached queries bearing that tag. The next read returns fresh data from the database.

9l. Dynamic Routes — App Manifest and App Pages

The Samantha sandbox uses Next.js dynamic segments to identify which app is being loaded. The route /store/apps/[appId] maps to any registered app. The appId is the app's UUID from the Vector database.

Dynamic segments support catch-all patterns ([...slug]) and optional catch-all ([[...slug]]). For Samantha, a user app may declare internal sub-routes (e.g., a multi-tab widget). The sandbox route could support this as /store/apps/[appId]/[[...appPath]], routing within the app's namespace without exposing Vector's internal routes.

9m. Parallel Routes and Intercepting Routes — UI Patterns for Apps

Two advanced routing features are directly useful for the Samantha developer experience:

  • Parallel Routes (@slot pattern): A layout can render multiple pages simultaneously in named slots. For Vector, this enables a split-pane dev view: @editor shows the app code, @preview shows the live output, both running in the same layout without a page refresh.
  • Intercepting Routes ((.)modal pattern): Clicking an app in the store can open a preview modal (intercepted route) without leaving the store page. Navigating directly to the URL renders the full-page app view instead. This is the standard Next.js pattern for modal-style previews.

9n. Image, Font, and Script Optimisation — Available to App Developers

Next.js built-in optimisations are available in the Samantha app environment, subject to CSP policy:

OptimisationHow it worksAvailable in apps?
<Image>Automatic WebP/AVIF conversion, lazy loading, size optimisationYes — app devs can use next/image
<Font>Google Fonts / local fonts downloaded at build time, no external request at runtimeYes — fonts used in app components inherit optimised loading
<Script>Third-party script loading with strategies: beforeInteractive, afterInteractive, lazyOnload, workerRestricted — scripts loaded by user apps must pass CSP nonce check; beforeInteractive is disallowed in sandbox context

9o. TypeScript — First-Class Support

Next.js auto-configures TypeScript. The next typegen command generates globally-available type helpers:

  • PageProps<'/store/apps/[appId]'> — typed props for any page including params
  • LayoutProps<'/store/apps'> — typed layout props including named slots
  • RouteContext<'/api/samantha/data/[type]'> — typed Route Handler context

This means Samantha SDK types can be auto-generated from the route structure itself, giving app developers full IntelliSense when using the SDK in TypeScript projects.

9p. Testing — Next.js Built-In Testing Support

Next.js integrates with Jest, Vitest, Playwright, and Cypress. The experimental unstable_doesProxyMatch utility allows unit-testing the Proxy configuration to verify that the sandbox route triggers auth checks and CSP header injection as expected. This is important for regression-testing permission changes without needing a full browser session.

9q. Next.js Ecosystem — Key Libraries for Samantha

LibraryPurposeRelevance to Samantha
JoseJWT sign/verify, Edge Runtime compatibleSession encryption for stateless JWT sessions; runs in Proxy without Node.js dependencies
ZodRuntime schema validationValidate all inputs to Samantha Route Handlers and Server Actions; prevent injection attacks
iron-sessionStateless session managementAlternative to custom JWT handling; simpler API for cookie-based encrypted sessions
server-onlyBuild-time guard for server modulesMark all Samantha SDK internals (DAL, permission checks, DB queries) as server-only
React cache()Deduplicate async calls within a render passWrap verifySession() and getSamanthaContext() to avoid redundant auth checks
Vercel AI SDKStreaming LLM responses via Route HandlersFuture: Samantha apps could include AI-generated data analysis widgets using Vector's data
NextAuth.js / Better AuthFull auth provider integrationIf Vector's auth layer is extended to support OAuth or social login, these integrate natively
OpenTelemetryDistributed tracing and observabilityInstrument the Samantha proxy API to trace per-app, per-user, per-resource-type call patterns; surfaced in Vector's dev diagnostics

9r. Mapping Next.js Primitives to Samantha Concepts

This table translates every relevant Next.js feature to its Samantha equivalent:

Next.js conceptSamantha equivalentWhere it lives
Server ComponentSandbox wrapper page — builds and injects samantha.app.Contextapp/store/apps/[appId]/page.js
Client ComponentUser app code — receives context as props, renders UI, calls Samantha SDKUser's app bundle
'use server' directiveSamantha write actions — all mutations go through a verified server functionapp/api/samantha/actions/route.js + Server Actions
Proxy (proxy.ts)Session presence check + CSP nonce generation + role-based redirectproxy.ts at project root
Data Access LayerverifySession() + getSamanthaContext() — the authoritative auth gatelib/samantha/dal.ts
use cacheCache portfolio data queries by tag; expire on writeInside Samantha data route handlers
revalidateTag()Expire stale cached data after any app-triggered mutationCalled from Samantha Server Actions after write
CSP noncePer-request cryptographic nonce injected into sandbox page — blocks injected scriptsGenerated in proxy.ts, passed as x-nonce header
server-onlyBuild-time guard preventing SDK internals from leaking to client bundlesImported at top of all DAL and session files
Dynamic Route [appId]Identifies which registered Vector app to loadapp/store/apps/[appId]/
Parallel Routes (@slot)Developer split-pane: code editor + live preview simultaneouslyDev-mode only layout
error.jsCatches unhandled errors from user app code without crashing the Vector hostapp/store/apps/[appId]/error.js
Route Handler streamingProgressive delivery of large data sets to user chart/report widgetsapp/api/samantha/data/route.js

9s. What Next.js Cannot Do — Gaps and Constraints

  • True process isolation: User app code runs in the same Node.js process as Vector. There is no VM-level sandboxing. The CSP + nonce model prevents browser-side attacks but does not sandbox Node.js server code. This means user apps must be client-side only (Client Components) — they cannot author Server Components or Server Actions in the same process. The Samantha SDK's server layer must be the only code that executes server-side on the user's behalf.
  • PPR incompatibility with nonce CSP: Partial Prerendering produces a static shell whose scripts cannot carry a per-request nonce. The sandbox page cannot use PPR — it must be fully dynamic.
  • Edge Runtime limitations: The Proxy (proxy.ts) runs on the Edge Runtime by default. Edge Runtime does not have access to the full Node.js API (no fs, limited crypto). Jose is compatible; bcrypt is not. Session verification in the Proxy must use Edge-compatible libraries.
  • WebSocket / real-time push: Next.js Route Handlers support streaming HTTP responses but do not natively support WebSockets. Real-time updates in Samantha apps require either polling, Server-Sent Events via a streaming Route Handler, or a separate WebSocket service.
